aliases: Egg Omelette with Onion and Chilli

english: Egg Omelette with Onion and Chilli

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic omelette class (FOODON_03302184). No specific Malaysian onion-chilli omelette entry exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked hen egg / omelette generically. No specific onion-chilli variant exists; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for onion and chilli omelette. Generic egg dish concepts are not sufficiently specific to warrant assignment.

serving.description: 1 omelette (approximately 2 eggs with onion and chilli)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one omelette made from approximately two eggs with sliced onion and chilli, weighing around 120 g.

nutrient_source.title: Egg omelette with vegetables — IMR Malaysia MyFCD / adapted from USDA FoodData Central: Egg, whole, cooked, omelet (FDC ID 173424) with onion and chilli adjustment
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) MyFCD; USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary anchor: USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 173424 (plain omelette, cooked). Values adjusted for the addition of onion (~15 g per 100 g final dish) and red chilli (~5 g per 100 g final dish), which marginally reduce energy density and add minor fibre and vitamin C. IMR MyFCD egg dish entries corroborate the macro range. The adaptation is conservative and within the expected variance of a two-egg omelette with vegetables.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — egg-based dishes; omelette analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for onion and chilli omelette. GI estimated at 32 based on plain egg omelette analogues in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (Diabetes Care). Eggs are a very low-GI food; onion and chilli additions do not materially raise GI. GL per serving calculated as (32 × 2.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 1.2 serving factor) / 100 ≈ 1.1, confirming very low glycemic load.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~210 mg per 100 g; ~252 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful of added salt during preparation; a 120 g serving contributes approximately 252 mg sodium, which is manageable within a 1500–2000 mg daily sodium target if other meals are low in sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~32) and low carbohydrate content (~2.8 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The onion and chilli omelette is a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low GI of approximately 32 and minimal carbohydrate load (~3.4 g per 120 g serving), posing negligible postprandial glucose impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dietary cholesterol (~320 mg per 100 g) and saturated fat (~3.1 g per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate consumption frequency; while eggs provide beneficial unsaturated fats and micronutrients, the cholesterol content (~384 mg per 120 g serving) warrants consideration within overall daily dietary cholesterol limits, and preparation oil choice should favour unsaturated oils.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Onion and Chilli Omelette is a simple, pan-fried egg dish popular in Malaysian and Singaporean hawker and kopitiam settings. It is prepared by beating whole eggs and pan-frying them with sliced onion and fresh or dried red chilli in a small amount of cooking oil, typically vegetable or palm oil, seasoned with salt. The result is a soft, lightly browned omelette with a savoury, mildly spicy flavour and a tender, slightly crispy edge. The dish is commonly served as a side dish alongside rice, roti, or noodles, or as a standalone protein-rich breakfast item. Nutritionally, it is a good source of complete protein (~10 g per 100 g), fat-soluble vitamins including vitamin A and vitamin D from the egg yolk, and B-vitamins particularly B12 and riboflavin. The carbohydrate content is very low (~2.8 g per 100 g), making it highly suitable for low-carbohydrate and diabetic-friendly meal plans. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 32 (low), and the glycemic load per serving is negligible at around 1.1. Sodium content is moderate and depends on the amount of salt added during cooking. The dish is naturally gluten-free, halal, and dairy-free, making it accessible to a wide range of dietary requirements. Clinically, it is a practical high-protein, low-GI option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or seeking weight management support, though those with cardiovascular disease should monitor overall egg consumption frequency due to dietary cholesterol.
common_user_queries: High protein low carb Malaysian food

confidence_reason: No exact entry for onion and chilli omelette was found in IMR MyFCD or HPB Foodo. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy plain omelette data (FDC ID 173424) with conservative adjustment for onion and chilli additions. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database egg-dish data. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source provides an exact match for this specific dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 173424 (plain omelette) with minor adjustments for onion and chilli; IMR MyFCD egg dish entries corroborate the macro range. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA source for a vegetable-oil-fried egg omelette — confirmed zero. 3. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate; no direct measurement for this specific dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value 4.5 estimated from USDA fatty acid profile for cooked egg omelette). 5. Cholesterol is high (~320 mg per 100 g) due to egg yolk content; individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate frequency. 6. The food_group 'Dairy and eggs' from the input is retained as eggs are the primary ingredient. 7. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03302184 and FoodEx2 A00LC are closest_parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for this specific dish. 8. SNOMED CT: no suitable specific concept identified; snomed_ct_match_type set to 'none'. 9. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean cuisine mandatory languages: English (food_name), Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi. 10. All aliases_by_language entries are confirmed present verbatim in the flat aliases list.
